projectContext.name: auth-hardening
projectContext.description: Add rate limiting and lockout to the login endpoint to resist credential stuffing attacks.
nonGoals: Do not refactor the existing session token logic
Do not add new database tables
Do not change the public API contract of /api/auth/login

phaseSpec.title: Rate limiting on login endpoint
phaseSpec.description: Add rate limiting to the POST /api/auth/login endpoint to cap attempts per identifier. Integrate with existing rate-limit middleware in src/middleware/ratelimit.ts.
acceptanceCriteria: Login endpoint returns 429 after threshold exceeded
Legitimate retries after cooldown window succeed

escalation.question: Should rate limiting key be per-IP address or per-username?
escalation.context: Existing ratelimit.ts middleware supports both modes via a `keyFn` parameter. Project plan says 'rate limit the login endpoint' without specifying granularity. Per-IP is simpler and matches existing patterns for /api/signup. Per-username catches credential stuffing where attacker rotates IPs but targets the same account, but requires reading the request body before rate limit check (inversion of current middleware order).
options: per-IP using x-forwarded-for header (matches existing /api/signup pattern)
per-username reading body.username (stronger for credential stuffing, requires middleware reorder)
composite: both keys, rate-limit if either exceeds (safest, 2x state)

projectContext.name: order-pipeline-v2
projectContext.description: Replace synchronous order processing with an async queue-backed pipeline.
nonGoals: Do not change the external Order API response format
Do not introduce new external service dependencies beyond the existing Redis instance
Do not remove the synchronous path (gate behind feature flag)

phaseSpec.title: Queue consumer idempotency
phaseSpec.description: Implement idempotency guarantees for the async order consumer so retried messages do not double-process.
acceptanceCriteria: Duplicate message delivery produces exactly one order record
Idempotency window is at least 24 hours

escalation.question: Two valid idempotency strategies surfaced during implementation. Which fits the project's non-goals and existing patterns?
escalation.context: Strategy A: use Redis SETNX with order_id as key, 24h TTL. Matches existing Redis usage in the codebase. Adds ~1ms per write. Risks: Redis eviction under memory pressure could allow duplicates. Strategy B: add an idempotency_key column to the orders table with a partial unique index, check on INSERT. More durable than Redis. Risks: adds a column, which the non-goals list may or may not forbid depending on interpretation — the non-goal 'do not introduce new external service dependencies beyond the existing Redis instance' speaks to services, not schema changes.
options: Strategy B (orders table column + partial unique index)

assumptions.description: Schema changes to existing tables are permitted under non-goals

projectContext.name: stripe-integration
projectContext.description: Integrate Stripe as a secondary payment processor alongside existing Braintree.
nonGoals: Do not change the checkout UI
Do not process real payments during development — use test mode only

phaseSpec.title: Stripe webhook handler
phaseSpec.description: Implement the /webhooks/stripe endpoint to handle payment_intent.succeeded and payment_intent.failed events, verify signatures, and update order state.
acceptanceCriteria: Handler verifies Stripe-Signature header per stripe-node docs
Successful events mark order as paid
Failed events mark order as payment_failed and emit retry event

escalation.question: Cannot test webhook signature verification end-to-end — STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET environment variable is missing from .env.test and is not in the password manager.
escalation.context: Signature verification code is written and unit tests pass with mocked signature. Full integration test requires the test-mode webhook secret from Stripe Dashboard > Developers > Webhooks. This appears to be a credential that was never provisioned for this development environment. Braintree equivalents live in 1Password 'shared-eng' vault but no Stripe entry exists there.

openQuestions: Where to obtain STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET for test-mode

projectContext.name: inventory-reconciliation
projectContext.description: Build nightly reconciliation job that compares warehouse inventory snapshots against the database and flags discrepancies.
nonGoals: Do not auto-correct discrepancies (read-only flagging only)
Do not alter the warehouse snapshot ingestion path
Do not block on slow warehouse systems — use 60s timeout

phaseSpec.title: Discrepancy detection algorithm
phaseSpec.description: Implement algorithm that diffs warehouse snapshot against inventory table and produces a list of mismatches. Must handle partial snapshots (some SKUs missing) and SKU aliases (one warehouse SKU maps to multiple database SKUs).
acceptanceCriteria: Diff produces list of {sku, warehouseQty, dbQty, delta} records
Partial snapshots flagged separately, not treated as zero-qty
SKU alias map consulted when direct match fails

executorCompletion.summary: Implemented diffInventory() with three-phase matching: direct SKU match, alias map match, partial-snapshot flagging.
executorCompletion.understanding: Goal is detecting inventory discrepancies without attempting correction. Alias handling and partial-snapshot detection are required.
assumptions: Alias map is loaded from config/sku-aliases.yml at job start
nonGoals: Did not modify warehouse snapshot ingestion

diffSummary: Added src/jobs/reconciliation/diffInventory.ts (~180 lines) with exported diffInventory(warehouse, db, aliases) function. Three internal helpers: directMatch, aliasMatch, partialSnapshotFlag. 12 unit tests cover direct/alias/partial/empty/null cases. Uses Map<string, Item> for O(1) lookups.

reviewerVerdicts.feedback: diffInventory() is O(n+m) as claimed but the aliases lookup in aliasMatch() is O(n) per unmatched SKU because it iterates the aliases Map. Worst case with many unmatched SKUs this is O(n²). Refactor to build a reverse-alias index at function entry (O(m)) then lookup O(1) per SKU. Also: test coverage does not include the 'same SKU aliased to multiple DB SKUs' case — the spec says this is possible.

Reverse-alias index added but the test for 'one warehouse SKU → multiple DB SKUs' now asserts quantity equality across all mapped DB SKUs, which is wrong: if warehouse reports 10 units of SKU-W and it aliases to SKU-A (dbQty 7) and SKU-B (dbQty 3), the sum matches (10 = 7+3) and should NOT be flagged. Current implementation flags it as a discrepancy because it compares warehouseQty to each dbQty individually. Fix the aggregation logic: for multi-aliased SKUs, sum dbQty across aliases before comparing.

projectContext.name: metrics-export
projectContext.description: Export application metrics to Prometheus for operational dashboards.
nonGoals: Do not add any new metric labels beyond those already documented
Do not change the metrics registry pattern used by existing services
Do not export sensitive data (PII, tokens, keys) in metric values or labels

phaseSpec.title: Request latency histogram
phaseSpec.description: Add a histogram metric 'http_request_duration_seconds' to capture request latency with labels {method, route, status_class}. Bucket boundaries per Prometheus best practices for web request latency.
acceptanceCriteria: Middleware records duration for every HTTP request
Buckets cover 10ms to 10s range with reasonable granularity

executorCompletion.summary: Added http_request_duration_seconds histogram with {method, route, status_class} labels via middleware.
executorCompletion.understanding: Capture latency per-request, categorize by status code class, expose via existing /metrics endpoint.
assumptions: Route label uses registered route pattern (/users/:id), not full URL path
nonGoals: Did not add new label keys beyond the three specified

diffSummary: Added histogram registration in src/metrics/registry.ts, middleware in src/middleware/metrics.ts. Buckets: [0.01, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2.5, 5, 10]. Labels populated from req.method, req.route?.path ?? req.path, and Math.floor(res.statusCode / 100) + 'xx'. 8 unit tests.

reviewerVerdicts.feedback: The fallback `req.route?.path ?? req.path` is a high-cardinality label hazard. When no route matches (404 path, typo'd URLs), req.path is the raw URL which can be unbounded — attackers hitting /foo?x=1, /foo?x=2, ... each create new time series. This is Prometheus's classic 'cardinality explosion' anti-pattern. For unmatched routes, use 'unknown' or the static string 'unmatched'.

Fallback changed to 'unknown', good. But now matched routes with path parameters still include raw parameter values in some frameworks where req.route.path isn't normalized. The test suite still uses Express which does normalize, but the middleware is advertised as framework-agnostic in the phase description. Either (a) scope to Express only and document the limitation, or (b) add a route normalizer that strips numeric IDs. Also: the phase spec says 'bucket boundaries per Prometheus best practices for web request latency' but the buckets chosen differ from Prometheus's recommended defaults (no 2.5 in default, etc). Either match Prometheus defaults or cite a best-practice source.
